questions.text: Which is the correct include guard for `student.hh`?
questions.explanation:
`#pragma once` is the standard modern way to prevent a header from being included more than once. It tells the compiler to include the file only the first time it is encountered.

Why the others are wrong:
- A) `#guard` / `#endguard` ÔÇö these preprocessor directives do not exist in C++.
- B) `#include_once` ÔÇö not a valid C++ directive.
- C) `#define STUDENT_HH` then `#undef STUDENT_HH` ÔÇö this defines then immediately undefines the macro, providing no protection at all.

The classical alternative is:
```cpp
#ifndef STUDENT_HH
#define STUDENT_HH
// declarations
#endif
```
but `#pragma once` is simpler and supported by all modern compilers.

questions.text: Which is true about the `this` pointer?

options.A: It must be declared as a parameter in every method
options.B: It points to the object that called the method
options.D: It points to the class definition, not an object

questions.text: What problem do include guards prevent?

options.A: Header files being modified by other files
options.B: Functions being called more than once at runtime
options.C: Same declarations processed twice, causing redefinition errors

questions.explanation:
When a header is included multiple times in the same translation unit (e.g. `a.hh` includes `b.hh` and `c.hh`, both of which include `student.hh`), the compiler processes it twice and sees the same declaration twice ÔÇö causing a **redefinition error**. Include guards prevent this.

```cpp
// Without guard: student.hh included twice Ôćĺ error: redefinition of 'struct Student'
// With #pragma once: second inclusion is silently skipped
```

Why the others are wrong:
- A) Include guards do not prevent modification of files.
- B) This is a runtime concept ÔÇö include guards operate at compile time, before any function calls.
- D) Linking errors come from missing definitions, not double inclusions.

questions.text: Which is **NOT** a benefit of .hh/.cpp separation?

options.A: Makes the program faster at runtime

questions.explanation:
Separating code into `.hh` (header) and `.cpp` (implementation) files is a **compile-time** technique ÔÇö it has no effect on the speed of the resulting executable at runtime.

The real benefits are:
- B) **Incremental compilation**: if you change `player.cpp`, only that file is recompiled.
- C) **Shared declarations**: any `.cpp` that `#include`s the header can use the declared types and functions.
- D) **Separation of concerns**: the header is the public interface; the `.cpp` contains the private implementation details.

questions.text: Which belongs in a .cpp file, **NOT** a .hh file?
questions.explanation:
Function **bodies** (the actual code that runs) belong in `.cpp` files. If a function body were placed in a header included by multiple `.cpp` files, the linker would see multiple definitions and produce an error.

What goes in a header (`.hh`):
- Structure and class definitions
- Function prototypes (declarations only)
- Include guards (`#pragma once`)

What goes in the implementation (`.cpp`):
- Function bodies
- `#include` for headers it needs
- Definitions of static/global variables

questions.text: What does `return *this;` do at the end of a method?

options.A: Deletes the object and frees memory
options.B: Returns a reference to the current object
options.C: Creates and returns a new copy of the object
options.D: Returns the address of the first member

questions.text: Given `Player p; Player* ptr = &p;`, which accesses `score`?
questions.explanation:
`ptr` is a **pointer** to a `Player`. To access a member through a pointer, use the arrow operator `->`. It is shorthand for `(*ptr).score`.

```cpp
Player p;
Player* ptr = &p;
ptr->score = 10;   // correct
(*ptr).score = 10; // also correct, but verbose
```

Why the others are wrong:
- A) `ptr.score` ÔÇö `.` works on values, not pointers. Compilation error.
- B) `ptr::score` ÔÇö `::` is for scope resolution (namespaces, classes), not for instances.
- D) `*ptr.score` ÔÇö operator precedence: `.` binds tighter than `*`, so this is `*(ptr.score)` ÔÇö trying to dereference `score`, not `ptr`.

options.D: A namespace cannot contain structs and functions

questions.explanation:
`Player` and `heal` are defined inside the `Game` namespace. Outside that namespace, they must be qualified with `Game::` or brought in with `using`.

```cpp
int main() {
    Game::Player hero = {"Warrior", 100}; // correct
    Game::heal(hero, 20);                  // correct
}
// or: using namespace Game; at the top
```

Why the others are wrong:
- B) Aggregate initialization `{"Warrior", 100}` is perfectly valid for structs.
- C) Reference parameters are legal everywhere ÔÇö this is a core C++ feature.
- D) Namespaces can contain any declarations, including structs, functions, and classes.

`Employee` has a nested `Department` member called `dept`. To reach `dept`'s `name`, chain the dot operator: `e.dept.name`.

```cpp
std::cout << e.dept.name; // prints "Engineering"
```

Why the others are wrong:
- A) `e.name` prints the employee's name ("Bob"), not the department name.
- C) `Department.name` ÔÇö `Department` is a type, not a variable. You cannot use `.` on a type.
- D) `e->dept.name` ÔÇö `->` is for pointers. `e` is a value, not a pointer.

questions.text: Which statement about structs is **FALSE**?

options.B: A function can return a struct by value
options.C: A struct must have a constructor to be used

questions.explanation:
Structs do **not** need a constructor. If none is defined, the compiler provides a default one, and aggregate initialization `{val1, val2}` works automatically.

```cpp
struct Point { int x; int y; };
Point p = {3, 7}; // no constructor needed
```

Why the others are true:
- A) In a `struct`, all members are `public` by default (unlike `class` where they are `private`).
- B) C++ structs are value types and can be returned by value just like `int` or `double`.
- D) `->` is exactly the member-access operator for pointers: `ptr->x` is `(*ptr).x`.

options.A: 100 ÔÇö the function received a copy
options.B: 0 ÔÇö the function modified the original
options.C: Undefined ÔÇö the struct is destroyed after the call
options.D: Compilation error ÔÇö structs cannot be passed

questions.explanation:
Aggregate initialization assigns values **in declaration order**. `Rect` declares `width` first, then `height`. So `{10, 20}` sets `width = 10` and `height = 20`.

```cpp
Rect r = {10, 20};
std::cout << r.width;  // 10
std::cout << r.height; // 20
```

The order of members in the struct definition is what matters, not anything else. This is a common trap: always check the struct definition to know which initializer maps to which field.

questions.text:
Which line calls the **assignment operator** (not copy constructor)?
```cpp
Vec a(1, 2);       // Line 1
Vec b = a;          // Line 2
Vec c(a);           // Line 3
Vec d(3, 4);       // Line 4
d = a;              // Line 5
```
questions.explanation:
The **assignment operator** (`operator=`) is called when you assign to an **already-existing** object. The **copy constructor** is called when a new object is created from an existing one.

- Line 1: `Vec a(1, 2)` ÔÇö parameterized constructor
- Line 2: `Vec b = a` ÔÇö looks like assignment but is actually **copy construction** (object `b` is being created here)
- Line 3: `Vec c(a)` ÔÇö explicitly calls the copy constructor
- Line 4: `Vec d(3, 4)` ÔÇö parameterized constructor
- **Line 5: `d = a`** ÔÇö `d` already exists, so this calls `operator=` Ôťô

The key rule: if the object is being **initialized** (created), it is the copy constructor. If it already exists, it is the assignment operator.

questions.text: **(multiple answers possible)** Which are part of the orthodox canonical form?
questions.explanation:
The **Rule of Three** (orthodox canonical form) states that if a class needs any one of these, it almost certainly needs all three:

1. **Copy constructor** ÔÇö `MyClass(const MyClass& other)` ÔÇö how to copy-construct from another instance
2. **Copy-assignment operator** ÔÇö `MyClass& operator=(const MyClass& other)` ÔÇö how to assign from another instance
3. **Destructor** ÔÇö `~MyClass()` ÔÇö how to clean up (free resources)

A **parameterized constructor** (B) is useful but not part of the canonical form ÔÇö it is not needed for correct copy/move/destruction behavior.

options.A: Constructor name must differ from class name
options.B: No error ÔÇö this is valid

options.D: `arr` is deleted, then `other.arr` (same memory) is read ÔÇö undefined behavior

questions.text: A class has `int* data` from `new`. What must a custom copy constructor do that the default does not?

options.A: Allocate new memory and copy the values
options.C: Delete the original's data before copying
options.D: Call the destructor of the source object

questions.explanation:
The **default copy constructor** performs a **shallow copy** ÔÇö it copies the pointer value, so both objects point to the same memory. When one of them is destroyed, it deletes the array, leaving the other with a dangling pointer.

A custom copy constructor must perform a **deep copy**: allocate fresh memory and copy the actual values.

```cpp
MyClass(const MyClass& other) {
    data = new int[other.size]; // allocate new memory
    for (int i = 0; i < other.size; i++)
        data[i] = other.data[i]; // copy the values
    size = other.size;
}
```

Why the others are wrong:
- B) Setting `data` to `nullptr` discards the data ÔÇö the copy would be empty and useless.
- C) You must never delete the original's data ÔÇö you are making a copy, not moving.
- D) Never call a destructor manually on a live object.

questions.text: In which order are destructors called for local variables?

options.A: Same order as creation (first created Ôćĺ first destroyed)
options.B: Reverse order (last created Ôćĺ first destroyed)
options.D: Undefined ÔÇö depends on the compiler

questions.explanation:
Local variables are destroyed in **reverse order of construction** ÔÇö last in, first out (LIFO), like a stack. This is guaranteed by the C++ standard.

```cpp
{
    A a; // constructed first
    B b; // constructed second
    C c; // constructed third
}     // c destroyed first, then b, then a
```

This matters when objects depend on each other: an object that uses another in its destructor must be destroyed first. The LIFO order ensures that dependencies are always cleaned up in the correct sequence.

questions.text: Which is the **postfix** increment operator?

options.B: `Num operator++(int) { Num old = *this; val++; return old; }`

questions.explanation:
The dummy `int` parameter distinguishes postfix from prefix:

- **Prefix** `++x`: `operator++()` ÔÇö no parameter, returns reference to modified self
- **Postfix** `x++`: `operator++(int)` ÔÇö dummy `int` parameter (never used), saves old value, increments, returns old value **by value**

```cpp
Num operator++(int) {   // postfix
    Num old = *this;    // save current state
    val++;              // increment
    return old;         // return what it was before
}
```

Postfix must return **by value** (not reference) because `old` is a local variable that would be destroyed at function exit.

questions.text: Which declares `operator<<` so `std::cout << point` works?

options.C: `friend std::ostream& operator<<(std::ostream& os, const Point& p);`

questions.text: Which pair is a valid function overload?
questions.explanation:
Function overloading requires the functions to differ in their **parameter types or count**. The return type alone is not enough.

- A) Same parameter (`int a`), only return type differs Ôćĺ **not valid** ÔÇö ambiguous
- B) `print(int)` vs `print(double)` ÔÇö different parameter type Ôćĺ **valid overload** Ôťô
- C) Identical signatures Ôćĺ **redefinition error**
- D) Same parameter (`int a`), only return type differs Ôćĺ **not valid**

```cpp
void print(int x)    { std::cout << x; }
void print(double x) { std::cout << x; }

print(42);    // calls print(int)
print(3.14);  // calls print(double)
```

questions.text: **(multiple answers possible)** Which operators CAN be overloaded?
questions.explanation:
Most C++ operators can be overloaded. The notable exceptions are: `.` (member access), `::` (scope resolution), `?:` (ternary), and `sizeof`.

- A) `+` Ôťô ÔÇö very commonly overloaded (e.g., `Vec + Vec`)
- B) `.` ÔťŚ ÔÇö **cannot** be overloaded; it is a language-level operator reserved for member access
- C) `==` Ôťô ÔÇö commonly overloaded for value comparison
- D) `<<` Ôťô ÔÇö overloaded for stream output (`std::cout << obj`)

Remember: overloading `.` would break the language's ability to access members, so it is intentionally forbidden.

questions.text: What does postfix `a++` return?
questions.explanation:
Postfix `a++` **increments `a`** but returns the **value it had before the increment**. This is why the overloaded postfix operator saves the old state before modifying.

```cpp
int a = 5;
int b = a++; // b = 5 (old value), a is now 6
int c = ++a; // c = 7 (new value), a is now 7
```

This distinction matters in loops and expressions. Prefer prefix `++a` when the old value is not needed ÔÇö it avoids creating a temporary copy.

`name` and `grade` are declared in the `private` section of the class ÔÇö they can only be accessed from within the class itself or from `friend` functions. The only way to access them from outside is through the public interface.

- A) `s.name` ÔÇö private member, compilation error
- B) `s.grade = 100` ÔÇö private member, compilation error
- C) `s.name = "Alice"` ÔÇö private member, compilation error
- D) `s.getName()` ÔÇö public method, calls the getter Ôćĺ compiles and prints `"Li Wei"` Ôťô

questions.text: Which method can be declared `const`?
questions.explanation:
A `const` method promises not to modify any member variables of the object. Only **read-only** methods (getters) can be `const`.

```cpp
int getAge() const { return age; } // correct: reads only
```

Why the others cannot be `const`:
- A) `setAge` writes to `age` ÔÇö modifies a member Ôćĺ cannot be `const`
- B) `reset` writes to `age` and `name` ÔÇö modifies members Ôćĺ cannot be `const`
- D) `increment` writes to `count` ÔÇö modifies a member Ôćĺ cannot be `const`

`const` methods are important: they allow your class to be used with `const` references and `const` objects.

options.A: `b.x` fails ÔÇö class members are private by default
options.C: `a.x` fails ÔÇö struct members are private by default
options.D: Both fail ÔÇö constructors are required first

questions.explanation:
The only difference between `struct` and `class` in C++ is the **default access level**:
- `struct` Ôćĺ members are **public** by default Ôćĺ `a.x = 10` is fine
- `class` Ôćĺ members are **private** by default Ôćĺ `b.x = 10` is a compilation error

```cpp
struct A { int x; };   // x is public
class  B { int x; };   // x is private

A a; a.x = 10; // OK
B b; b.x = 10; // Error: 'x' is private
```

To fix: add `public:` before `int x;` in class B, or change `class` to `struct`.

options.C: `radius` should be initialized in the class definition
options.D: Constructors have no return type ÔÇö remove `void`

options.A: `seconds = seconds` assigns parameter to itself ÔÇö member never set
options.B: Parameter cannot share a name with a member
options.C: Must use an initializer list instead
options.D: No error ÔÇö member is correctly set

questions.text: A `Student` needs an address (city, zip) and a name. An `Address` class exists. Which design is best?
questions.explanation:
Use **composition** (has-a) when one object contains another as a component. A student *has an* address ÔÇö it does not *inherit from* address.

- B) `Student` contains an `Address` object Ôćĺ correct Ôťô
- A) Duplicating `city` and `zip` directly in `Student` breaks the reuse of the `Address` class and couples Student to address internals.
- C) `Student : public Address` (inheritance) means "Student is an Address" ÔÇö that is not true. Inheritance models is-a relationships. This is a design error.
- D) `std::string data[3]` loses all type safety and structure ÔÇö the meaning of each element is unclear.

options.A: Grade being read by other classes

questions.explanation:
The setter validates its input before storing it. Only values in the range [0, 100] are accepted ÔÇö any other value is silently rejected and `grade` remains unchanged.

```cpp
student.setGrade(85);  // OK ÔÇö stored
student.setGrade(-5);  // rejected ÔÇö grade unchanged
student.setGrade(110); // rejected ÔÇö grade unchanged
```

This is **encapsulation** in practice: by making `grade` private and routing all writes through a setter, the class guarantees its invariant (grade is always valid). If `grade` were public, any code could set it to `-999`.
